title: Databases and Persistence
summary: Connect Java applications to relational databases using JDBC and connection pooling

sections.title: Databases as a Filing Cabinet System
sections.content: A file cabinet has labeled drawers (schemas), folders inside (tables), and documents with structured fields (rows and columns). Relational databases organize data the same way but with superpowers: instant search, relationships between tables, and ACID transactions that prevent data corruption. Java connects to databases via JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) — a standard API that works with any database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, H2, etc.) by using the appropriate driver. The driver translates JDBC calls into the database's native protocol, like a universal remote that works with any TV brand.

sections.title: Connecting and Querying with JDBC
sections.content: This code connects to a database, runs a query, and processes results using a PreparedStatement

sections.title: Connection Pooling and ORMs
sections.content: Opening a database connection is expensive — like making a phone call that takes 2 seconds to connect. If every web request opens a new connection, your database quickly becomes overwhelmed. Connection pooling (HikariCP is the industry standard) keeps a pool of permanent open connections, like a taxi stand with cars ready to go. When your code asks for a connection, it gets one from the pool instantly; when done, the connection returns to the pool rather than closing. ORMs (Object-Relational Mappers) like Hibernate or jOOQ automate the mapping between Java objects and database tables — generating SQL for you. The trade-off is that ORMs hide complexity but add a learning curve and can generate inefficient queries.

sections.title: SQL injection via string concatenation in queries
sections.content: Most common mistake: building SQL by concatenating strings: String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + userName + "'". A malicious userName like "' OR '1'='1" deletes all data or bypasses authentication. Fix: always use PreparedStatement with ? placeholders. Wrong: Statement stmt = conn.createStatement(); stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id);. Correct: PreparedStatement stmt = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?"); stmt.setInt(1, id);

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Create a ProductRepository class that connects to an H2 in-memory database. Implement methods: createTable() that creates a products table (id, name, price, quantity), insertProduct(Product p), findById(int id), and findAll(). Use PreparedStatement for all queries. Test by inserting 3 products, finding one by ID, and listing all.
hints: What JDBC URL connects to an in-memory H2 database?
What method of PreparedStatement executes an INSERT?
How do you map a ResultSet row back to a Product object?
